"free trial" that requires customers to register payment details, and immediately charge the customer to turn the "free trial" into an active subscription as soon as the period ends.

paragraphs.text: There are a number of patterns I'd like to bring attention: -- "Gacha" games -- Widespread amongst "free-to-play" games today are highly predatory designs and practices. The most egregious among these is a genre of games commonly referred to as "gacha". These games are glorified gambling software, engineered to trick customers into overspending and, most particularly, massively abusing the "FOMO" (Fear Of Missing Out) human bias by constantly running limited-time goods and events. These games systematically demand daily time investment, and/or money to catch up. (For the record, these games also systematically used digital currency systems to obfuscate the real prices of transactions). These practices have boomed in the last decade and have become well-documented and researched. Below is one of the many studies done on them: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9389446/ by Tang ACY, Lee PH, Lam SC, Siu SCN, Ye CJ, Lee RL. "Prediction of problem gambling by demographics, gaming behavior and psychological correlates among gacha gamers: A cross-sectional online survey in Chinese young adults. Front Psychiatry. 2022 Aug 5;13:940281. doi: 10.3389/fpsyt.2022.940281. PMID: 35990074; PMCID: PMC9389446." (Not included in attachments as the file is too heavy) This is an entire market built upon predatory practices, and thus needs particular considerations. --Online subscriptions-- A large number of online services offer subscriptions with a "free trial" that requires customers to register payment details, and immediately charge the customer to turn the "free trial" into an active subscription as soon as the period ends. This practice is widespread and attempts to extort money out of customers who would forget to end their trial on time. I'd like the DFA to enforce an end to this practice, for example, by requiring a confirmation from the customer to charge money and start a subscription upon a trial's end. --Subscriptions for non-service products-- Some companies, including big industry players (Adobe for example) have recently begun selling offers that are disproportionately unfair to customers. These offers entail no longer selling their software as-is, but instead selling a subscription that grants customers the temporary right to use said software. This kind of offer has always been a thing for software that functions on a seller's servers, but is now being applied to software that runs on the customer's computer. In practice, this means customers are robbed of their ownership and access to software installed on their own computer; Translated to the physical world, this would be equivalent to bricking a customer's light bulb if the customer does not pay a subscription... I'd like the DFA to enforce the customer's right to buy permanent ownership of a copy of the software, for any software that runs on the customer's machine and does not incur operating costs for the seller.
